text.DRB: Be you also ready: for at what hour you think not, the Son of man will come.

text.NRSV: Peter said, “Lord, are you telling this parable for us or for everyone?”
text.Rotherham: But Peter said—Lord! unto us, this parable, speakest thou? or, even unto all?
text.KJV: ¶ Then Peter said vnto him, Lord, speakest thou this parable vnto vs, or euen to all?
text.NABRE: Then Peter said, “Lord, is this parable meant for us or for everyone?”
text.DRB: And Peter said to him: Lord, doest thou speak this parable to us, or likewise to all?

text.NRSV: And the Lord said, “Who then is the faithful and prudent manager whom his master will put in charge of his slaves, to give them their allowance of food at the proper time?
text.Rotherham: And the Lord said—Who then is the faithful steward, the prudent one, whom the lord will appoint over his body of attendants, to be giving, in due season, the measured allowance of wheat?
text.KJV: And the Lord said, Who then is that faithfull and wise steward, whom his Lord shall make ruler ouer his houshold, to giue them their portion of meate in due season?
text.NABRE: And the Lord replied, “Who, then, is the faithful and prudent steward whom the master will put in charge of his servants to distribute [the] food allowance at the proper time?
text.DRB: And our Lord said: Who (thinkest thou) is a faithful steward and wise, whom the Lord appointeth over his family, to give them in season their measure of wheat?

text.NRSV: Blessed is that slave whom his master will find at work when he arrives.
text.Rotherham: Happy, that servant, whom the lord, when he cometh shall find doing, thus!
text.KJV: Blessed is that seruant, whom his Lord when he commeth, shall find so doing.
text.NABRE: Blessed is that servant whom his master on arrival finds doing so.
text.DRB: Blessed is that servant, whom when the Lord cometh, he shall find so doing.

text.NRSV: Truly I tell you, he will put that one in charge of all his possessions.
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, I say unto you—Over all his possessions, will he appoint him.
text.KJV: Of a trueth, I say vnto you, that hee will make him ruler ouer all that he hath.
text.NABRE: Truly, I say to you, he will put him in charge of all his property.
text.DRB: Verily I say to you, that over all things which he possesseth, he shall appoint him.

text.NRSV: But if that slave says to himself, ‘My master is delayed in coming,’ and if he begins to beat the other slaves, men and women, and to eat and drink and get drunk,
text.Rotherham: But, if that servant should say in his heart—My lord delayeth to come! and should begin to be striking the youths and the maidens,—to be eating also, and drinking, and making himself drunk,
text.KJV: But and if that seruant say in his heart, My Lord delayeth his comming and shall beginne to beat the men seruants, and maidens, and to eate and drinke, and to be drunken:
text.NABRE: But if that servant says to himself, ‘My master is delayed in coming,’ and begins to beat the menservants and the maidservants, to eat and drink and get drunk,
text.DRB: But if that servant say in his heart, My Lord is long a coming; and shall begin to strike the servants and handmaids, and eat and drink, and be drunk:

text.NRSV: the master of that slave will come on a day when he does not expect him and at an hour that he does not know, and will cut him in pieces, and put him with the unfaithful.
text.Rotherham: The lord of that servant, will have come—on a day when he is not expecting, and in an hour when he is not taking note,—and will cut him asunder, and, his part, with the unfaithful, will appoint.
text.KJV: The Lord of that seruant will come in a day when hee looketh not for him, and at an houre when hee is not ware, and will cut him in sunder, and will appoint him his portion with the vnbeleeuers.
text.NABRE: then that servant’s master will come on an unexpected day and at an unknown hour and will punish him severely and assign him a place with the unfaithful.
text.DRB: the Lord of that servant shall come in a day that he hopeth not, and at an hour that he knoweth not, and shall divide him, and shall appoint his portion with the infidels.

text.NRSV: That slave who knew what his master wanted, but did not prepare himself or do what was wanted, will receive a severe beating.
text.Rotherham: And, that servant, who had come to know the will of his lord, and neither prepared, nor wrought unto his will, shall be beaten with many stripes;
text.KJV: And that seruant which knew his Lords will, and prepared not himselfe, neither did according to his will, shalbe beaten with many stripes.
text.NABRE: That servant who knew his master’s will but did not make preparations nor act in accord with his will shall be beaten severely;
text.DRB: And that servant that knew the will of his Lord, and prepared not himself, & did not according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

text.NRSV: But the one who did not know and did what deserved a beating will receive a light beating. From everyone to whom much has been given, much will be required; and from the one to whom much has been entrusted, even more will be demanded.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, he who had not come to know, and did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. And, every one to whom was given much, much, shall be sought from him; and, he to whom they committed much, for more than common, will they ask him.
text.KJV: But hee that knew not, and did commit things worthy of stripes, shall bee beaten with few stripes. For vnto whomsoeuer much is giuen, of him shal bee much required: and to whom men haue committed much, of him they will aske the more.
text.NABRE: and the servant who was ignorant of his master’s will but acted in a way deserving of a severe beating shall be beaten only lightly. Much will be required of the person entrusted with much, and still more will be demanded of the person entrusted with more. Jesus: A Cause of Division.
text.DRB: But he that knew not, and did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few. And every one to whom much was given, much shall be required of him: and to whom they committed much, more will they demand of him.

text.NRSV: “I came to bring fire to the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!
text.Rotherham: Fire, came I to cast upon the earth,—and what can I wish, if, already, it hath been, kindled?
text.KJV: ¶ I am come to send fire on the earth, and what will I, if it be already kindled?
text.NABRE: “I have come to set the earth on fire, and how I wish it were already blazing!
text.DRB: I came to cast fire on the earth; & what will I, but that it be kindled?

text.NRSV: I have a baptism with which to be baptized, and what stress I am under until it is completed!
text.Rotherham: But, an immersion, have I, to be immersed with, and how am I distressed, until it be ended!
text.KJV: But I haue a baptisme to be baptized with, and how am I straitned till it be accomplished?
text.NABRE: There is a baptism with which I must be baptized, and how great is my anguish until it is accomplished!
text.DRB: But I have to be baptized with a baptism: and how am I straitened until it be dispatched?

text.NRSV: Do you think that I have come to bring peace to the earth? No, I tell you, but rather division!
text.Rotherham: Suppose ye, that, peace, I am come to give in the earth? Nay, I tell you, but rather division.
text.KJV: Suppose yee that I am come to giue peace on earth? I tell you, Nay, but rather diuision.
text.NABRE: Do you think that I have come to establish peace on the earth? No, I tell you, but rather division.
text.DRB: Think you that I came to give peace on the earth? No, I tell you, but separation.

text.NRSV: From now on five in one household will be divided, three against two and two against three;
text.Rotherham: For there shall be, henceforth, five in one house, divided,—three against two, and two against three:
text.KJV: For from henceforth there shalbe fiue in one house diuided, three against two, and two against three.
text.NABRE: From now on a household of five will be divided, three against two and two against three;
text.DRB: For there shall be from this time, five in one house divided: three against two, and two against three.

text.NRSV: they will be divided: father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law.”
text.Rotherham: There shall be divided—Father against son, and son against father, mother against daughter, and daughter against the mother,—mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law, and daughter-in-law against the mother-in-law.
text.KJV: The father shall bee diuided against the sonne, and the sonne against the father: the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother: the mother in lawe against her daughter in lawe, and the daughter in law against her mother in lawe.
text.NABRE: a father will be divided against his son and a son against his father, a mother against her daughter and a daughter against her mother, a mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.” Signs of the Times.
text.DRB: There shall be divided, the father against the son, and the son against his father, the mother against the daughter, and the daughter against the mother, the mother in law against her daughter in law, and the daughter in law against her mother in law.

text.NRSV: He also said to the crowds, “When you see a cloud rising in the west, you immediately say, ‘It is going to rain’; and so it happens.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to say, even unto the multitudes,—Whensoever ye see a cloud springing up from the west, straightway, ye are saying—A thunderstorm is coming! and it happeneth thus.
text.KJV: ¶ And he said also to the people, When ye see a cloud rise out of the West, straightway yee say, There commeth a showre, and so it is.
text.NABRE: He also said to the crowds, “When you see [a] cloud rising in the west you say immediately that it is going to rain—and so it does;
text.DRB: And he said also to the multitudes, when you see a cloud rising from the west, by and by you say, A shower cometh, and so it cometh to pass:

text.NRSV: And when you see the south wind blowing, you say, ‘There will be scorching heat’; and it happens.
text.Rotherham: And, whensoever a south wind, blowing, ye say—A scorching heat, will there be! and it cometh to pass.
text.KJV: And when ye see the Southwind blow, ye say, There will be heat, and it commeth to passe.
text.NABRE: and when you notice that the wind is blowing from the south you say that it is going to be hot—and so it is.
text.DRB: and when the south wind blowing, you say, That there will be heat: & it cometh to pass.

text.NRSV: You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of earth and sky, but why do you not know how to interpret the present time?
text.Rotherham: Hypocrites! the face of the earth and of the heaven, ye know how to scan; but, this season, how know ye not to scan;
text.KJV: Ye hypocrites, ye can discerne the face of the skie, and of the earth: but how is it that yee doe not discerne this time?
text.NABRE: You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of the earth and the sky; why do you not know how to interpret the present time? Settlement with an Opponent.
text.DRB: Hypocrites, the face of the Heaven and of the earth you have skill to discern: but this time how do you not discern?

text.NRSV: “And why do you not judge for yourselves what is right?
text.Rotherham: Why, moreover, even from yourselves, judge ye not what is just?
text.KJV: Yea, and why euen of your selues iudge ye not what is right?
text.NABRE: “Why do you not judge for yourselves what is right?
text.DRB: And why of yourselves also judge you not that which is just?

text.NRSV: Thus, when you go with your accuser before a magistrate, on the way make an effort to settle the case, or you may be dragged before the judge, and the judge hand you over to the officer, and the officer throw you into prison.
text.Rotherham: For, as thou art going along with thine adversary unto a ruler, on the way, take pains to get a release from him; lest once he drag thee along unto the judge, and, the judge, deliver thee up to the punisher,—and, the punisher, cast thee into prison:
text.KJV: ¶ When thou goest with thine aduersary to the magistrate, as thou art in the way, giue diligence that thou mayest be deliuered from him, lest hee hale thee to the Iudge, and the Iudge deliuer thee to the officer, and the officer cast thee into prison.
text.NABRE: If you are to go with your opponent before a magistrate, make an effort to settle the matter on the way; otherwise your opponent will turn you over to the judge, and the judge hand you over to the constable, and the constable throw you into prison.
text.DRB: And when thou goest with thy adversary to the Prince, in the way endeavour to be delivered from him: lest perhaps he draw thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the exactor, and the exactor cast thee into prison.

text.NRSV: I tell you, you will never get out until you have paid the very last penny.”
text.Rotherham: I tell thee—In nowise shalt thou come out from thence, until, even the last fraction, thou pay!
text.KJV: I tell thee, Thou shalt not depart thence, till thou hast payd the very last mite.
text.NABRE: I say to you, you will not be released until you have paid the last penny.”
text.DRB: Isay to thee, thou shalt not go out thence, until thou pay the very last mite.

text.NRSV: At that very time there were some present who told him about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
text.Rotherham: Now there were present some, in that very season, bringing tidings to him, concerning the Galilaeans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
text.KJV: There were present at that season, some that told him of the Galileans, whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
text.NABRE: Chapter 13 A Call to Repentance. At that time some people who were present there told him about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with the blood of their sacrifices.
text.DRB: And there were certain present at that very time telling him of the Galilæans, whose blood Pilate mingled with their sacrifices.

text.NRSV: He asked them, “Do you think that because these Galileans suffered in this way they were worse sinners than all other Galileans?
text.Rotherham: And, answering, he said unto them—Suppose ye, that, these Galilaeans, had become, sinners beyond all the Galilaeans, because, these things, they have suffered?
text.KJV: And Iesus answering, said vnto them, Suppose ye that these Galileans were sinners aboue all the Galileans, because they suffered such things?
text.NABRE: He said to them in reply, “Do you think that because these Galileans suffered in this way they were greater sinners than all other Galileans?
text.DRB: And he answering said to them: Think you that these Galilæans were sinners more than all the Galilæans, that they suffered such things?

text.NRSV: No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all perish as they did.
text.Rotherham: Nay! I tell you, but, except ye repent, ye, all, in like manner, shall perish.
text.KJV: I tell you, Nay: but except yee repent, ye shall all likewise perish.
text.NABRE: By no means! But I tell you, if you do not repent, you will all perish as they did!
text.DRB: No, I say to you: but unless you have penance, you shall all likewise perish.

text.NRSV: Or those eighteen who were killed when the tower of Siloam fell on them—do you think that they were worse offenders than all the others living in Jerusalem?
text.Rotherham: Or, those eighteen, upon whom fell the tower in Siloam, and slew them, suppose ye that, they, had become, debtors, beyond all the men who were dwelling in Jerusalem?
text.KJV: Or those eighteene, vpon whom the towre in Siloe fell, and slew them, thinke ye that they were sinners aboue all men that dwelt in Hierusalem?
text.NABRE: Or those eighteen people who were killed when the tower at Siloam fell on them —do you think they were more guilty than everyone else who lived in Jerusalem?
text.DRB: As those eighteen upon whom the tower fell in Siloe, and slew them: think you that they also were debters above all the men that dwell in Jerusalem?

text.NRSV: No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all perish as they did.”
text.Rotherham: Nay! I tell you, but, except ye repent, ye, all, in the same way, shall perish.
text.KJV: I tell you, Nay; but except yee repent, ye shall all likewise perish.
text.NABRE: By no means! But I tell you, if you do not repent, you will all perish as they did!” The Parable of the Barren Fig Tree.
text.DRB: No, I say to you: but if you have not penance you shall all likewise perish.

text.NRSV: Then he told this parable: “A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came looking for fruit on it and found none.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to speak this parable:—A certain man had, a fig-tree, planted in his vineyard, and he canto seeking fruit therein, and found none.
text.KJV: ¶ Hee spake also this parable, A certaine man had a figtree planted in his Uineyard, and he came and sought fruit thereon, and found none.
text.NABRE: And he told them this parable: “There once was a person who had a fig tree planted in his orchard, and when he came in search of fruit on it but found none,
text.DRB: And he said this similitude: A certain man had a figtree planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking for fruit on it, and found not.

text.NRSV: So he said to the gardener, ‘See here! For three years I have come looking for fruit on this fig tree, and still I find none. Cut it down! Why should it be wasting the soil?’
text.Rotherham: And he said unto the vine-dresser—Lo! three years, I come, seeking fruit in this fig-tree, and find none. Cut it down! Why doth it make, even the round useless?
text.KJV: Then said hee vnto the dresser of his Uineyard, Beholde, these three yeeres I come seeking fruit on this figtree, and finde none: cut it downe, why cumbreth it the ground?
text.NABRE: he said to the gardener, ‘For three years now I have come in search of fruit on this fig tree but have found none. [So] cut it down. Why should it exhaust the soil?’
text.DRB: And he said to the dresser of the vineyard, Lo it is three years since I come seeking for fruit upon this figtree, and I find not. Cut it down therefore; whereto doth it also occupy the ground?

text.NRSV: He replied, ‘Sir, let it alone for one more year, until I dig around it and put manure on it.
text.Rotherham: And, he, answering, saith unto him—Sir! let it alone this year also, until such time as I dig about it, and throw in manure,—
text.KJV: And he answering, said vnto him, Lord, let it alone this yeere also, till I shall digge about it, and doung it:
text.NABRE: He said to him in reply, ‘Sir, leave it for this year also, and I shall cultivate the ground around it and fertilize it;
text.DRB: But he answering said to him: Lord, let it alone this year also, until I dig about it, and dung it.

text.NRSV: If it bears fruit next year, well and good; but if not, you can cut it down.’”
text.Rotherham: and if it may bear fruit for the future; …but, otherwise, certainly, thou shalt cut it down.
text.KJV: And if it beare fruit, Well: and if not, then after that, thou shalt cut it downe.
text.NABRE: it may bear fruit in the future. If not you can cut it down.’” Cure of a Crippled Woman on the Sabbath.
text.DRB: And if happily it yield fruit: but if not, hereafter thou shalt cut it down.

text.NRSV: Now he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath.
text.Rotherham: And he was teaching in one of the synagogues, on the Sabbath.
text.KJV: And he was teaching in one of the Synagogues on the Sabbath.
text.NABRE: He was teaching in a synagogue on the sabbath.
text.DRB: And he was teaching in their Synagogue on the Sabboths.

text.NRSV: And just then there appeared a woman with a spirit that had crippled her for eighteen years. She was bent over and was quite unable to stand up straight.
text.Rotherham: And lo! a woman, having a spirit of weakness eighteen years,—and was bowed together, and unable to lift herself up, at all.
text.KJV: ¶ And beholde, there was a woman which had a spirit of infirmitie eighteene yeeres, and was bowed together, and could in no wise lift vp her selfe.
text.NABRE: And a woman was there who for eighteen years had been crippled by a spirit; she was bent over, completely incapable of standing erect.
text.DRB: And behold a woman that had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years: and she was crooked, neither could she look upward at all.

text.NRSV: When Jesus saw her, he called her over and said, “Woman, you are set free from your ailment.”
text.Rotherham: And, seeing her, Jesus called her and said to her—Woman! thou art loosed from thy weakness,—
text.KJV: And when Iesus saw her, he called her to him, and said vnto her, Woman, thou art loosed frō thy infirmitie.
text.NABRE: When Jesus saw her, he called to her and said, “Woman, you are set free of your infirmity.”
text.DRB: Whom when Jesus saw, he called her unto him, and said to her: Woman, thou art delivered from thy infirmity.

text.NRSV: When he laid his hands on her, immediately she stood up straight and began praising God.
text.Rotherham: and laid on her his hands; and, instantly, she was made straight again, and began glorifying God.
text.KJV: And hee layd his handes on her, and immediatly she was made straight, and glorified God.
text.NABRE: He laid his hands on her, and she at once stood up straight and glorified God.
text.DRB: And he imposed hands upon her, and forthwith she was made straight and glorified God.

text.NRSV: But the leader of the synagogue, indignant because Jesus had cured on the sabbath, kept saying to the crowd, “There are six days on which work ought to be done; come on those days and be cured, and not on the sabbath day.”
text.Rotherham: But the synagogue-ruler, answering, being greatly displeased that, on the Sabbath, Jesus had healed, began saying unto the multitude—Six days, there are, in which men ought to get their work done; On them, therefore, come and be healed, and, not on the day of rest.
text.KJV: And the ruler of the Synagogue answered with indignation, because that Iesus had healed on the Sabbath day, and said vnto the people, There are sixe dayes in which men ought to worke: in them therefore come and be healed, and not on the Sabbath day.
text.NABRE: But the leader of the synagogue, indignant that Jesus had cured on the sabbath, said to the crowd in reply, “There are six days when work should be done. Come on those days to be cured, not on the sabbath day.”
text.DRB: And the Archsynagogue answering (because he had indignation that Jesus had cured on the Sabboth) said to the multitude: Six days there are wherein you ought to work. In them therefore come, and be cured; and not in the Sabboth day.

text.NRSV: But the Lord answered him and said, “You hypocrites! Does not each of you on the sabbath untie his ox or his donkey from the manger, and lead it away to give it water?
text.Rotherham: The Lord answered him, and said—Hypocrites! Doth not, each one of you, on the Sabbath, loose his ox or ass from the manger, and, leading it away, give it drink?
text.KJV: The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doeth not each one of you on the Sabbath loose his oxe or his asse from the stall, and leade him away to watering?
text.NABRE: The Lord said to him in reply, “Hypocrites! Does not each one of you on the sabbath untie his ox or his ass from the manger and lead it out for watering?
text.DRB: And our Lord answering to him, said: Hypocrite, doth not every one of you upon the Sabboth loose his ox or his ass from the manger, and leadeth them to water?

text.NRSV: And ought not this woman, a daughter of Abraham whom Satan bound for eighteen long years, be set free from this bondage on the sabbath day?”
text.Rotherham: But, this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan had bound, lo! eighteen years, was there not a needs-be that she should be loosed from this bond, on the day of rest?
text.KJV: And ought not this woman being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, loe these eighteene yeeres, be loosed from this bond on the Sabbath day?
text.NABRE: This daughter of Abraham, whom Satan has bound for eighteen years now, ought she not to have been set free on the sabbath day from this bondage?”
text.DRB: But this daughter of Abraham whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, ought not she to be loosed from this bond on the Sabboth day?

text.NRSV: When he said this, all his opponents were put to shame; and the entire crowd was rejoicing at all the wonderful things that he was doing.
text.Rotherham: And, as he was saying, these, things, all who had been setting themselves against him were being put to shame; and, all the multitude, were rejoicing over all the glorious things which were being brought to pass by him.
text.KJV: And when hee had said these things, all his aduersaries were ashamed: & all the people reioyced for all the glorious things that were done by him.
text.NABRE: When he said this, all his adversaries were humiliated; and the whole crowd rejoiced at all the splendid deeds done by him. The Parable of the Mustard Seed.
text.DRB: And when he said these things, all his adversaries were ashamed: and all the people rejoiced in all things that were gloriously done of him.

text.NRSV: He said therefore, “What is the kingdom of God like? And to what should I compare it?
text.Rotherham: He went on to say, therefore—Whereunto, is the kingdom of God, like? And, whereunto, shall I liken it?
text.KJV: ¶ Then said he, Unto what is the kingdome of God like? and whereunto shall I resemble it?
text.NABRE: Then he said, “What is the kingdom of God like? To what can I compare it?
text.DRB: He said therefore: Whereunto is the Kingdom of God like, and whereunto shall I esteem it like?

text.NRSV: It is like a mustard seed that someone took and sowed in the garden; it grew and became a tree, and the birds of the air made nests in its branches.”
text.Rotherham: It is, like, unto a grain of mustard seed, which a man took and cast into his own garden; and it grew, and became a tree, and, the birds of heaven, lodged amongst its branches.
text.KJV: It is like a graine of mustard seed, which a man tooke, and cast into his garden, and it grew, and waxed a great tree: and the foules of the aire lodged in the branches of it.
text.NABRE: It is like a mustard seed that a person took and planted in the garden. When it was fully grown, it became a large bush and ‘the birds of the sky dwelt in its branches.’” The Parable of the Yeast.
text.DRB: It is like to a mustard seed, which a man took and cast into his garden and it grew, and became a great tree, & the fowls of the air rested in the boughs thereof.

text.NRSV: And again he said, “To what should I compare the kingdom of God?
text.Rotherham: And, again, he said—Whereunto, shall I liken the kingdom of God?
text.KJV: And againe hee said, Whereunto shall I liken the kingdome of God?
text.NABRE: Again he said, “To what shall I compare the kingdom of God?
text.DRB: And again he said: Like to what shall I esteem the Kingdom of God?

text.NRSV: It is like yeast that a woman took and mixed in with three measures of flour until all of it was leavened.”
text.Rotherham: It is, like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour, until, the whole, was leavened.
text.KJV: It is like leauen, which a woman tooke and hidde in three measures of meale, till the whole was leauened.
text.NABRE: It is like yeast that a woman took and mixed [in] with three measures of wheat flour until the whole batch of dough was leavened.” The Narrow Door; Salvation and Rejection.
text.DRB: It is like to leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened.

text.NRSV: Jesus went through one town and village after another, teaching as he made his way to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: And he was journeying on, city by city and village by village, and making, progress, unto Jerusalem.
text.KJV: And he went thorow the cities and villages, teaching and iourneying towards Hierusalem.
text.NABRE: He passed through towns and villages, teaching as he went and making his way to Jerusalem.
text.DRB: And he went by the cities and towns teaching, and making his journey unto Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Someone asked him, “Lord, will only a few be saved?” He said to them,
text.Rotherham: And one said unto him—Lord! are they, few, who are being saved? And, he, said unto them—
text.KJV: Then said one vnto him, Lord, are there few that be saued? And he said vnto them,
text.NABRE: Someone asked him, “Lord, will only a few people be saved?” He answered them,
text.DRB: And a certain man said to him: Lord, be they few that are saved? But he said to them:

text.NRSV: “Strive to enter through the narrow door; for many, I tell you, will try to enter and will not be able.
text.Rotherham: Be striving to enter through the narrow door; for, many, I say unto you, will seek to enter, and will not be able,—
text.KJV: ¶ Striue to enter in at the strait gate: for many, I say vnto you, will seeke to enter in, and shall not be able.
text.NABRE: “Strive to enter through the narrow door, for many, I tell you, will attempt to enter but will not be strong enough.
text.DRB: Strive to enter by the narrow gate: because many, I say to you, shall seek to enter, and shall not be able.

text.NRSV: When once the owner of the house has got up and shut the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock at the door, saying, ‘Lord, open to us,’ then in reply he will say to you, ‘I do not know where you come from.’
text.Rotherham: when once the householder shall rouse himself, and lock the door, and ye begin to stand, outside, and to knock at the door, saying—Lord! open to us; and he shall answer and say unto you—I know you not, whence ye are.
text.KJV: When once the master of the house is risen vp, & hath shut to the doore, and ye begin to stand without, & to knocke at the doore, saying, Lord, Lord, open vnto vs, and he shal answere, & say vnto you, I know you not whence you are:
text.NABRE: After the master of the house has arisen and locked the door, then will you stand outside knocking and saying, ‘Lord, open the door for us.’ He will say to you in reply, ‘I do not know where you are from.’
text.DRB: But when the good-man of the house shall enter in, and shut the door, and you shall begin to stand without, and knock at the door, saying, Lord open to us: and he answering shall say to you, I know you not whence you are:

text.NRSV: Then you will begin to say, ‘We ate and drank with you, and you taught in our streets.’
text.Rotherham: Then, will ye begin to say—We did eat and drink in thy presence, and, in our broadways, thou didst, teach;
text.KJV: Then shall ye begin to say, Wee haue eaten and drunke in thy presence, and thou hast taught in our streets.
text.NABRE: And you will say, ‘We ate and drank in your company and you taught in our streets.’
text.DRB: then you shall begin to say: We did eat before thee and drink, and in our streets didst thou teach.

text.NRSV: But he will say, ‘I do not know where you come from; go away from me, all you evildoers!’
text.Rotherham: And he will speak, saying unto you—I know not whence ye are; Depart from me, all workers of unrighteous—
text.KJV: But he shall say, I tell you, I know you not whence you are; depart from me all ye workers of iniquitie.
text.NABRE: Then he will say to you, ‘I do not know where [you] are from. Depart from me, all you evildoers!’
text.DRB: And he shall say to you, I know you not whence you are, depart from me all ye workers of iniquity.

text.NRSV: There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth when you see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves thrown out.
text.Rotherham: There, will be weeping and gnashing of teeth, as soon as ye see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets, in the kingdom of God, and, yourselves, being thrust forth outside.
text.KJV: There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when yee shall see Abraham, and Isaac, and Iacob, and all the Prophets in the kingdome of God, and you your selues thrust out.
text.NABRE: And there will be wailing and grinding of teeth when you see Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God and you yourselves cast out.
text.DRB: There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth: when you shall see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob, and all the Prophets in the Kingdom of God, and you to be thrust out.

text.NRSV: Then people will come from east and west, from north and south, and will eat in the kingdom of God.
text.Rotherham: And they shall have come from east and west, and from north and south, and be made recline in the kingdom of God.
text.KJV: And they shall come from the East, and from the West, and from the North, and from the South, and shall sit downe in the kingdome of God.
text.NABRE: And people will come from the east and the west and from the north and the south and will recline at table in the kingdom of God.
text.DRB: And there shall come from the East and West & the North and the South; and shall sit down in the Kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: Indeed, some are last who will be first, and some are first who will be last.”
text.Rotherham: And lo! there are, last, who shall be, first, and there are, first, who shall be, last;
text.KJV: And behold, there are last, which shall be first; and there are first, which shall be last.
text.NABRE: For behold, some are last who will be first, and some are first who will be last.”
text.DRB: And behold, they are last that shall be first, and they be first that shall be last.

text.NRSV: At that very hour some Pharisees came and said to him, “Get away from here, for Herod wants to kill you.”
text.Rotherham: In that very hour, came near certain Pharisees, saying unto him, Go forth, and be journeying hence, because, Herod, desireth to slay thee.
text.KJV: ¶ The same day there came certaine of the Pharises, saying vnto him, Get thee out, and depart hence; for Herode will kill thee.
text.NABRE: Herod’s Desire to Kill Jesus. At that time some Pharisees came to him and said, “Go away, leave this area because Herod wants to kill you.”
text.DRB: The same day there came certain of the Pharisees, saying to him: Depart and get thee hence, because Herod will kill thee.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “Go and tell that fox for me, ‘Listen, I am casting out demons and performing cures today and tomorrow, and on the third day I finish my work.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—Go and tell this fox, Lo! I am casting out demons, and, cures, am I finishing, to-day, and to-morrow,—
text.KJV: And he said vnto them, Go ye and tell that Foxe, behold, I cast out deuils, and I doe cures to day and to morrow, and the third day I shall be perfected.
text.NABRE: He replied, “Go and tell that fox, ‘Behold, I cast out demons and I perform healings today and tomorrow, and on the third day I accomplish my purpose.
text.DRB: And he said to them: Go, and tell that fox, Behold I cast out devils, and perfit cures this day and tomorrow, and the third day I am consummate.

text.NRSV: Yet today, tomorrow, and the next day I must be on my way, because it is impossible for a prophet to be killed outside of Jerusalem.’
text.Rotherham: and, on the third, I am to be made perfect. Nevertheless, I must needs, to-day, and tomorrow, and the following, be journeying on, because it is impossible that a prophet perish, outside Jerusalem.
text.KJV: Neuerthelesse, I must walke to day and to morrow, and the day following: for it cannot be that a Prophet perish out of Hierusalem.
text.NABRE: Yet I must continue on my way today, tomorrow, and the following day, for it is impossible that a prophet should die outside of Jerusalem.’ The Lament over Jerusalem.
text.DRB: But yet I must walk this day and tomorrow and the day following, because it cannot be that a Prophet perish out of Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often have I desired to gather your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!
text.Rotherham: Jerusalem! Jerusalem! she that slayeth the prophets, and stoneth them that are sent unto her! How often, have I desired to gather together thy children, like as a hen, her own brood, under her wings,—and ye did not desire!
text.KJV: O Hierusalem, Hierusalem, which killest the Prophets, and stonest them that are sent vnto thee; how often would I haue gathered thy children together, as a henne doeth gather her brood vnder her wings, & ye would not?
text.NABRE: “Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how many times I yearned to gather your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, but you were unwilling!
text.DRB: Jerusalem, Jerusalem which killest the Prophets, and stonest them that are sent to thee, how often would I gather thy children as the bird doth her brood under her wings, and thou wouldest not?

text.NRSV: See, your house is left to you. And I tell you, you will not see me until the time comes when you say, ‘Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.’”
text.Rotherham: Lo! your house is left to you. [And] I say unto you—In nowise shall ye see me, until ye say,—Blessed, is he that cometh in the name of the Lord!
text.KJV: Behold, your house is left vnto you desolate. And verely I say vnto you, ye shall not see me, vntill the time come when yee shall say, Blessed is hee that commeth in the Name of the Lord.
text.NABRE: Behold, your house will be abandoned. [But] I tell you, you will not see me until [the time comes when] you say, ‘Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.’”
text.DRB: Behold your house shall be left desert to you. And I say to you, that you shall not see me till it come when you shall say: Blessed is he that cometh in the name of our Lord.

text.NRSV: On one occasion when Jesus was going to the house of a leader of the Pharisees to eat a meal on the sabbath, they were watching him closely.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he entered into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees, on a Sabbath, to eat bread, that, they, were narrowly watching him.
text.KJV: And it came to passe, as he went into the house of one of the chief Pharises to eat bread on þ Sabbath day, that they watched him.
text.NABRE: Chapter 14 Healing of the Man with Dropsy on the Sabbath. On a sabbath he went to dine at the home of one of the leading Pharisees, and the people there were observing him carefully.
text.DRB: And it came to pass when Jesus entered into the house of a certain Prince of the Pharisees upon the Sabboth to eat bread, and they watched him.

text.NRSV: Just then, in front of him, there was a man who had dropsy.
text.Rotherham: And lo! there was, a certain man, who had the dropsy, before him.
text.KJV: And behold, there was a certaine man before him, which had the dropsie.
text.NABRE: In front of him there was a man suffering from dropsy.
text.DRB: And behold there was a certain man before him that had the dropsy.

text.NRSV: And Jesus asked the lawyers and Pharisees, “Is it lawful to cure people on the sabbath, or not?”
text.Rotherham: And Jesus, answering, spake unto the Lawyers and Pharisees, saying—Is it allowed, on the Sabbath, to cure, or not? But, they, held their peace.
text.KJV: And Iesus answering, spake vnto the Lawyers and Pharises, saying, Is it lawfull to heale on the Sabbath day?
text.NABRE: Jesus spoke to the scholars of the law and Pharisees in reply, asking, “Is it lawful to cure on the sabbath or not?”
text.DRB: And Jesus answering, spake to the Lawyers and Pharisees, saying: Is it Lawful to cure on the Sabboth?

text.NRSV: But they were silent. So Jesus took him and healed him, and sent him away.
text.Rotherham: And taking hold [of him] he healed and dismissed him,—
text.KJV: And they held their peace. And he tooke him, and healed him, & let him go,
text.NABRE: But they kept silent; so he took the man and, after he had healed him, dismissed him.
text.DRB: But they held their peace. But he taking him, healed him, and sent him away.

text.NRSV: Then he said to them, “If one of you has a child or an ox that has fallen into a well, will you not immediately pull it out on a sabbath day?”
text.Rotherham: and, unto them, said—Which of you, shall have a son or an ox that, into a pit, shall fall, and will not straightway pull him up, on the day of rest?
text.KJV: And answered them, saying, Which of you shall haue an asse or an oxe fallen into a pit, and will not straightway pull him out on the Sabbath day?
text.NABRE: Then he said to them, “Who among you, if your son or ox falls into a cistern, would not immediately pull him out on the sabbath day?”
text.DRB: And answering them he said: Which of you shall have an ass or an ox fallen into a pit; and will not incontinent draw him out on the Sabboth day?

text.NRSV: And they could not reply to this.
text.Rotherham: And they could not return an answer unto these things.
text.KJV: And they could not answere him againe to these things.
text.NABRE: But they were unable to answer his question. Conduct of Invited Guests and Hosts.
text.DRB: And they could not answer him to these things.

text.NRSV: When he noticed how the guests chose the places of honor, he told them a parable.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to speak, unto the invited, a parable,—observing how, the first couches, they were choosing; saying unto them—
text.KJV: ¶ And he put foorth a parable to those which were bidden, when he marked howe they chose out the chiefe roumes, saying vnto them,
text.NABRE: He told a parable to those who had been invited, noticing how they were choosing the places of honor at the table.
text.DRB: And he spake to them also that were invited a parable, marking how they chose the first seats at the table, saying to them:

text.NRSV: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding banquet, do not sit down at the place of honor, in case someone more distinguished than you has been invited by your host;
text.Rotherham: Whensoever thou hast been invited by anyone unto a marriage feast, do not recline on the first couch; lest once, a more honourable than thou, have been invited by him,
text.KJV: When thou art bidden of any man to a wedding, sit not downe in the highest roume: lest a more honourable man then thou be bidden of him,
text.NABRE: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding banquet, do not recline at table in the place of honor. A more distinguished guest than you may have been invited by him,
text.DRB: When thou art invited to a marriage, sit not down in the first place, lest perhaps a more honourable than thou be invited of him;

text.NRSV: and the host who invited both of you may come and say to you, ‘Give this person your place,’ and then in disgrace you would start to take the lowest place.
text.Rotherham: and he that invited both thee and him should come, and say unto thee—Give, unto this one, place! and, then, thou shouldst begin, with shame, the last place, to occupy.
text.KJV: And hee that bade thee and him, come, and say to thee, Giue this man place: and thou begin with shame to take the lowest roume.
text.NABRE: and the host who invited both of you may approach you and say, ‘Give your place to this man,’ and then you would proceed with embarrassment to take the lowest place.
text.DRB: and he that bade thee and him, come and say to thee, Give this man place: and then thou begin with shame to take the last place.

text.NRSV: But when you are invited, go and sit down at the lowest place, so that when your host comes, he may say to you, ‘Friend, move up higher’; then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at the table with you.
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever thou hast been invited, pass on and fall back into the last place, that, whensoever he that hath invited thee shall come, he may say unto thee—Friend! come close up, higher. Then, shalt thou have honour before all who are reclining together with thee.
text.KJV: But when thou art bidden, goe and sit downe in the lowest roume, that when he that bade thee commeth, hee may say vnto thee, Friend, goe vp higher: then shalt thou haue worship in the presence of them that sit at meate with thee.
text.NABRE: Rather, when you are invited, go and take the lowest place so that when the host comes to you he may say, ‘My friend, move up to a higher position.’ Then you will enjoy the esteem of your companions at the table.
text.DRB: But when thou art bidden, go, sit down in the lowest place: that when he that invited thee cometh, he may say to thee, Friend sit up higher: then shalt thou have glory before them that sit at table with thee.

text.NRSV: For all who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted.”
text.Rotherham: Because, everyone who exalteth himself, shall be abased; and, he that abaseth himself, shall be exalted?
text.KJV: For whosoeuer exalteth himselfe, shalbe abased: and hee that humbleth himselfe, shalbe exalted.
text.NABRE: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.”
text.DRB: because every one that exalteth himself, shall be humbled: and he that humbleth himself, shall be exalted.

text.NRSV: He said also to the one who had invited him, “When you give a luncheon or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, in case they may invite you in return, and you would be repaid.
text.Rotherham: Moreover he went on to say, unto him also who had invited him,—Whensoever thou mayest be making a dinner or a supper, do not call thy friends, or thy brothers, or thy kinsfolk, or rich neighbours,—lest once, they also, invite thee in return, and it become a recompense unto thee.
text.KJV: ¶ Then said hee also to him that bade him, When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, neither thy kinsemen, nor thy rich neighbours, lest they also bid thee againe, and a recompence be made thee.
text.NABRE: Then he said to the host who invited him, “When you hold a lunch or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or your wealthy neighbors, in case they may invite you back and you have repayment.
text.DRB: And he said to him also that had invited him: When thou makest a dinner or a supper, call not thy friends, nor thy brethren, nor kinsmen, nor thy neighbours that are rich: lest perhaps they also invite thee again, and recompence be made to thee.

text.NRSV: But when you give a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind.
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever, an entertainment, thou mayest be making, invite the destitute, the tried, the lame, the blind;
text.KJV: But when thou makest a feast, call the poore, the maimed, the lame, the blinde,
text.NABRE: Rather, when you hold a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind;
text.DRB: But when thou makest a feast, call the poor, feeble, lame, and blind,

text.NRSV: And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you, for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”
text.Rotherham: and, happy, shalt thou be, that they have not wherewith to recompense thee, for it shall be recompensed unto thee, in the resurrection of the righteous.
text.KJV: And thou shalt be blessed, for they cannot recompense thee: for thou shalt be recompensed at the resurrection of the iust.
text.NABRE: blessed indeed will you be because of their inability to repay you. For you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.” The Parable of the Great Feast.
text.DRB: and thou shalt be blessed, because they have not to recompence thee: for recompence shall be made thee in the resurrection of the just.

text.NRSV: One of the dinner guests, on hearing this, said to him, “Blessed is anyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!”
text.Rotherham: And one of those reclining together, hearing these things, said unto him—Happy, whoever shall eat bread in the kingdom of God!
text.KJV: ¶ And when one of them that sate at meate with him, heard these things, he said vnto him, Blessed is hee that shall eate bread in the kingdom of God.
text.NABRE: One of his fellow guests on hearing this said to him, “Blessed is the one who will dine in the kingdom of God.”
text.DRB: When one of them that sat at the table with him, had heard these things, he said to him: Blessed is he that shall eat bread in the Kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: Then Jesus said to him, “Someone gave a great dinner and invited many.
text.Rotherham: But, he, said unto him—A certain man, was making a great supper, and invited many;
text.KJV: Then said hee vnto him, A certaine man made a great supper, and bade many:
text.NABRE: He replied to him, “A man gave a great dinner to which he invited many.
text.DRB: But he said to him: A certain man made a great supper, and called many.

text.NRSV: At the time for the dinner he sent his slave to say to those who had been invited, ‘Come; for everything is ready now.’
text.Rotherham: and he sent out his servant, at the hour of the supper, to say unto the invited—Be coming! because, even now, is it, ready.
text.KJV: And sent his seruant at supper time, to say to them that were bidden, Come, for all things are now ready.
text.NABRE: When the time for the dinner came, he dispatched his servant to say to those invited, ‘Come, everything is now ready.’
text.DRB: And he sent his servant at the hour of supper to say to the invited, that they should come, because now all things are ready.

text.NRSV: But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a piece of land, and I must go out and see it; please accept my apologies.’
text.Rotherham: And they all began, one after another to excuse themselves. The first, said unto him—A field, have I bought, and have need to go out and see it: I request thee, hold me excused.
text.KJV: And they all with one consent began to make excuse: The first said vnto him, I haue bought a piece of ground, and I must needs goe and see it: I pray thee haue me excused.
text.NABRE: But one by one, they all began to excuse themselves. The first said to him, ‘I have purchased a field and must go to examine it; I ask you, consider me excused.’
text.DRB: And they began all at once to make excuse. The first said to him, I have bought a farm, and I must needs go forth and see it; I pray thee hold me excused.

text.NRSV: Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out; please accept my apologies.’
text.Rotherham: And, another, said—Five yoke of oxen, have I bought, and am going my way to prove them; I request thee, hold me excused.
text.KJV: And another said, I haue bought fiue yoke of oxen, and I goe to prooue them: I pray thee haue me excused.
text.NABRE: And another said, ‘I have purchased five yoke of oxen and am on my way to evaluate them; I ask you, consider me excused.’
text.DRB: And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to prove them; I pray thee, hold me excused.

text.NRSV: Another said, ‘I have just been married, and therefore I cannot come.’
text.Rotherham: And, another, said—A wife, have I married, and, for this cause, I cannot come!
text.KJV: And another said, I haue maried a wife: and therefore I cannot come.
text.NABRE: And another said, ‘I have just married a woman, and therefore I cannot come.’
text.DRB: And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.

text.NRSV: So the slave returned and reported this to his master. Then the owner of the house became angry and said to his slave, ‘Go out at once into the streets and lanes of the town and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
text.Rotherham: And, going near, the servant reported unto his lord these things. Then, provoked to anger, the master of the house said unto his servant—Go out quickly, into the broadways and streets of the city,—and, the destitute, and tried, and blind, and lame, bring thou in here.
text.KJV: So that seruant came, and shewed his lord these things. Then the master of the house being angry, sayde to his seruant, Goe out quickely into the streetes and lanes of the city, and bring in hither the poore, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blinde.
text.NABRE: The servant went and reported this to his master. Then the master of the house in a rage commanded his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the town and bring in here the poor and the crippled, the blind and the lame.’
text.DRB: And the servant returning told these things to his Lord. Then the Master of the house being angry, said to his servant: Go forth quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and the poor and feeble and blind and lame bring in hither.

text.NRSV: And the slave said, ‘Sir, what you ordered has been done, and there is still room.’
text.Rotherham: And the servant said—Lord! what thou didst order, hath been done; and, yet, there is, room.
text.KJV: And the seruant said, Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is roume.
text.NABRE: The servant reported, ‘Sir, your orders have been carried out and still there is room.’
text.DRB: And the servant said: Lord, it is done as thou didst command, and yet there is place.

text.NRSV: Then the master said to the slave, ‘Go out into the roads and lanes, and compel people to come in, so that my house may be filled.
text.Rotherham: And the lord said unto the servant—Go out among the highways and fences, and compel them to come in; that my house may be filled.
text.KJV: And the Lord said vnto the seruant, Goe out into the high wayes and hedges, and compell them to come in, that my house may be filled.
text.NABRE: The master then ordered the servant, ‘Go out to the highways and hedgerows and make people come in that my home may be filled.
text.DRB: And the Lord said to the servant: Go forth into the ways and hedges; and compel them to enter, that my house may be filled.

text.NRSV: For I tell you, none of those who were invited will taste my dinner.’”
text.Rotherham: For, I say unto you—Not one of those men who had been invited, shall taste of my supper.
text.KJV: For I say vnto you, that none of those men which were bidden, shall taste of my supper.
text.NABRE: For, I tell you, none of those men who were invited will taste my dinner.’” Sayings on Discipleship.
text.DRB: But I say to you, that none of those men that were called, shall taste my supper.

text.NRSV: Now large crowds were traveling with him; and he turned and said to them,
text.Rotherham: And there were journeying together with him many multitudes; and, turning, he said unto them—
text.KJV: ¶ And there went great multitudes with him: and hee turned, and said vnto them,
text.NABRE: Great crowds were traveling with him, and he turned and addressed them,
text.DRB: And great multitudes went with him; and turning, he said to them:

text.NRSV: “Whoever comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and even life itself, cannot be my disciple.
text.Rotherham: If anyone cometh unto me, and hateth not his own father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brothers, and sisters, further also, even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
text.KJV: If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea and his owne life also, hee cannot be my disciple.
text.NABRE: “If any one comes to me without hating his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
text.DRB: If any man come to me and hateth not his father and mother, and wife and children, and brethren and sisters, yea and his own life besides; he cannot be my disciple.

text.NRSV: Whoever does not carry the cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
text.Rotherham: Whoever beareth not his own cross, and cometh after me, cannot be my disciple.
text.KJV: And whosoeuer doeth not beare his crosse, and come after me, cannot be my disciple.
text.NABRE: Whoever does not carry his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple.
text.DRB: And he that doth not bear his cross and come after me; cannot be my Disciple.

text.NRSV: For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not first sit down and estimate the cost, to see whether he has enough to complete it?
text.Rotherham: For, who from among you, wishing to build a tower, doth not first sit down and count the cost,—whether he hath sufficient for completion;
text.KJV: For which of you intending to build a towre, sitteth not downe first, and counteth the cost, whether he haue sufficient to finish it?
text.NABRE: Which of you wishing to construct a tower does not first sit down and calculate the cost to see if there is enough for its completion?
text.DRB: For, which of you minding to build a tower, doth not first sit down and reckon the charges that are necessary, whether he have to finish it:

text.NRSV: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it will begin to ridicule him,
text.Rotherham: lest once, he having laid a foundation, and not being able to finish, all who are looking on, should begin to mock at him,
text.KJV: Lest haply after hee hath laide the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that behold it, begin to mock him,
text.NABRE: Otherwise, after laying the foundation and finding himself unable to finish the work the onlookers should laugh at him
text.DRB: lest, after that he hath laid the foundation, and is not able to finish it, all that see it, begin to mock him,

text.NRSV: saying, ‘This person began to build and was not able to finish.’
text.Rotherham: saying—This man, began to build, and was not able to finish!
text.KJV: Saying, This man beganne to build, and was not able to finish.
text.NABRE: and say, ‘This one began to build but did not have the resources to finish.’
text.DRB: saying, That this man began to build, and he could not finish it?

text.NRSV: Or what king, going out to wage war against another king, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to oppose the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?
text.Rotherham: Or, what king, moving on to encounter, another king, in battle, will not sit down first and take counsel, whether he is able, with ten thousand, to meet him who, with twenty thousand, is coming against him.
text.KJV: Or what king going to make war against another king, sitteth not downe first, and consulteth whether he be able with ten thousand, to meete him that commeth against him with twentie thousand?
text.NABRE: Or what king marching into battle would not first sit down and decide whether with ten thousand troops he can successfully oppose another king advancing upon him with twenty thousand troops?
text.DRB: Or what king about to go to make war against another king, doth not first sit down and think whether he be able with ten thousands to meet him that with twenty thousands cometh against him?

text.NRSV: If he cannot, then, while the other is still far away, he sends a delegation and asks for the terms of peace.
text.Rotherham: And, if not, by any means, while he is yet afar off, he sendeth, an embassy, and requesteth the conditions of peace.
text.KJV: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, hee sendeth an ambassage, and desireth conditions of peace.
text.NABRE: But if not, while he is still far away, he will send a delegation to ask for peace terms.
text.DRB: Otherwise whiles he is yet far off, sending a legacy he asketh those things that belong to peace.

text.NRSV: So therefore, none of you can become my disciple if you do not give up all your possessions.
text.Rotherham: Thus, therefore, everyone from amongst you, who doth not bid adieu unto all his own possessions, cannot be my disciple.
text.KJV: So likewise, whosoeuer he be of you, that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple.
text.NABRE: In the same way, everyone of you who does not renounce all his possessions cannot be my disciple. The Simile of Salt.
text.DRB: So therefore every one of you that doth not renounce all that he possesseth, cannot be my disciple.

text.NRSV: “Salt is good; but if salt has lost its taste, how can its saltiness be restored?
text.Rotherham: Good, therefore is the salt; but, if, even the salt, become tasteless, wherewith shall it be seasoned?
text.KJV: ¶ Salt is good: but if the salt haue lost his sauour, wherewith shall it be seasoned?
text.NABRE: “Salt is good, but if salt itself loses its taste, with what can its flavor be restored?
text.DRB: Salt is good. But if the salt leese his virtue, wherewith shall it be seasoned?

text.NRSV: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; they throw it away. Let anyone with ears to hear listen!”
text.Rotherham: Neither for land nor for manure, is it, fit: outside, they cast it! He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.
text.KJV: It is neither fit for the land, nor yet for the dunghill: but men cast it out. He that hath eares to heare, let him heare.
text.NABRE: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; it is thrown out. Whoever has ears to hear ought to hear.”
text.DRB: It is profitable neither for the ground, nor for the dunghill, but it shall be cast forth. He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

text.NRSV: Now all the tax collectors and sinners were coming near to listen to him.
text.Rotherham: But all the tax-collectors and the sinners were, unto him, drawing near, to be hearkening unto him;
text.KJV: Then drew neere vnto him all the Publicanes and sinners, for to heare him.
text.NABRE: Chapter 15 The Parable of the Lost Sheep. The tax collectors and sinners were all drawing near to listen to him,
text.DRB: And there approached Publicans and sinners unto him for to hear him.

text.NRSV: And the Pharisees and the scribes were grumbling and saying, “This fellow welcomes sinners and eats with them.”
text.Rotherham: and both the Pharisees and the Scribes were murmuring, saying, This man, unto sinners, giveth welcome, and eateth with them.
text.KJV: And the Pharises and Scribes murmured, murmured, saying, This man receiueth sinners, and eateth with them.
text.NABRE: but the Pharisees and scribes began to complain, saying, “This man welcomes sinners and eats with them.”
text.DRB: And the Pharisees and the Scribes murmured saying: That this man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them.

text.NRSV: So he told them this parable:
text.Rotherham: And he spake unto them this parable, saying—
text.KJV: ¶ And he spake this parable vnto them, saying,
text.NABRE: So to them he addressed this parable.
text.DRB: And he spake to them this parable, saying:

text.NRSV: “Which one of you, having a hundred sheep and losing one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness and go after the one that is lost until he finds it?
text.Rotherham: What man from among you, having a hundred sheep, and losing, from among them, one, doth not leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness and go his way after the lost one, until he find it?
text.KJV: What man of you hauing an hundred sheepe, if he loose one of them, doth not leaue the ninety and nine in the wildernesse, and goe after that which is lost, vntill he find it?
text.NABRE: “What man among you having a hundred sheep and losing one of them would not leave the ninety-nine in the desert and go after the lost one until he finds it?
text.DRB: What man of you having an hundred sheep: and if he hath lost one of them, doth he not leave the ninety nine in the desert, and goeth after that which was lost until he find it?

text.NRSV: When he has found it, he lays it on his shoulders and rejoices.
text.Rotherham: And, finding it, he layeth it upon his shoulders, rejoicing;
text.KJV: And when he hath found it, hee layeth it on his shoulders, reioycing.
text.NABRE: And when he does find it, he sets it on his shoulders with great joy
text.DRB: And when he hath found it, layeth it upon his shoulders rejoicing:

text.NRSV: And when he comes home, he calls together his friends and neighbors, saying to them, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost.’
text.Rotherham: and, coming unto his house, calleth together the friends and the neighbours, saying unto them—Rejoice with me! because I have found my sheep that was lost!
text.KJV: And when he commeth home, he calleth together his friends, and neighbours, saying vnto them, Reioyce with me, for I haue found my sheepe which was lost.
text.NABRE: and, upon his arrival home, he calls together his friends and neighbors and says to them, ‘Rejoice with me because I have found my lost sheep.’
text.DRB: and coming home calleth together his friends and neighbours, saying to them: Rejoice with me, because I have found my sheep that was lost?

text.NRSV: Just so, I tell you, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.
text.Rotherham: I say unto you—Thus, joy in heaven, will there be, over one sinner repenting, rather than over ninety-nine righteous persons, who indeed have, no need, of repentance.
text.KJV: I say vnto you, that likewise ioy shall be in heauen ouer one sinner that repenteth, more then ouer ninety and nine iust persons, which need no repentance.
text.NABRE: I tell you, in just the same way there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous people who have no need of repentance.
text.DRB: Isay to you, that even so there shall be joy in Heaven upon one sinner that doth penance, than upon ninety nine just that need not penance.

text.NRSV: “Or what woman having ten silver coins, if she loses one of them, does not light a lamp, sweep the house, and search carefully until she finds it?
text.Rotherham: Or, what woman, having, ten pieces of silver, if she lose one piece, doth not light a lamp, and sweep the house, and seek carefully, until she find it?
text.KJV: ¶ Either what woman hauing ten pieces of siluer, if she lose one piece, doth not light a candle, and sweepe the house, and seeke diligently till shee find it?
text.NABRE: The Parable of the Lost Coin. “Or what woman having ten coins and losing one would not light a lamp and sweep the house, searching carefully until she finds it?
text.DRB: Or what woman having ten groats: if she leese one groat, doth she not light a candle, and sweep the house, and seek diligently, until she find?

text.NRSV: When she has found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found the coin that I had lost.’
text.Rotherham: And, having found it, she calleth together her female friends and neighbours, saying—Rejoice with me! because I have found the piece of silver which I had lost.
text.KJV: And when she hath found it, she calleth her friends and her neighbours together, saying, Reioyce with me, for I haue found þ piece which I had lost.
text.NABRE: And when she does find it, she calls together her friends and neighbors and says to them, ‘Rejoice with me because I have found the coin that I lost.’
text.DRB: And when she hath found, calleth together her friends and neighbours, saying: Rejoice with me, because I have found the groat which I had lost?

text.NRSV: Just so, I tell you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner who repents.”
text.Rotherham: Thus, I say unto you, there ariseth joy in presence of the messengers of God, over one sinner repenting.
text.KJV: Likewise I say vnto you, there is ioy in the presence of the Angels of God, ouer one sinner that repenteth.
text.NABRE: In just the same way, I tell you, there will be rejoicing among the angels of God over one sinner who repents.”
text.DRB: So I say to you, there shall be joy before the Angels of God upon one sinner that doth penance.

text.NRSV: Then Jesus said, “There was a man who had two sons.
text.Rotherham: And he said—A certain man, had two sons.
text.KJV: ¶ And hee said, A certaine man had two sonnes:
text.NABRE: The Parable of the Lost Son. Then he said, “A man had two sons,
text.DRB: And he said: A certain man had two sons;

text.NRSV: The younger of them said to his father, ‘Father, give me the share of the property that will belong to me.’ So he divided his property between them.
text.Rotherham: And the younger of them said unto the father—Father! give me the share that falleth to me, of what there is. And, he, divided unto them the living.
text.KJV: And the yonger of them said to his father, Father, giue me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he diuided vnto them his liuing.
text.NABRE: and the younger son said to his father, ‘Father, give me the share of your estate that should come to me.’ So the father divided the property between them.
text.DRB: and the younger of them said to his father: Father, give me the portion of substance that belongeth to me. And he divided unto them the substance.

text.NRSV: A few days later the younger son gathered all he had and traveled to a distant country, and there he squandered his property in dissolute living.
text.Rotherham: And, after not many days, the younger son, gathering all together, left home for a country far away, and, there, squandered his substance with riotous living.
text.KJV: And not many dayes after, the yonger sonne gathered al together, and tooke his iourney into a farre countrey, and there wasted his substance with riotous liuing.
text.NABRE: After a few days, the younger son collected all his belongings and set off to a distant country where he squandered his inheritance on a life of dissipation.
text.DRB: And not many days after the younger son gathering all his things together went from home into a far country: and there he wasted his substance, living riotously.

text.NRSV: When he had spent everything, a severe famine help up throughout that country, and he began to be in need.
text.Rotherham: And, when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine throughout that country,—and, he, began to be in want.
text.KJV: And when he had spent all, there arose a mighty famine in that land, and he beganne to be in want.
text.NABRE: When he had freely spent everything, a severe famine struck that country, and he found himself in dire need.
text.DRB: And after he had spent all, there fell a sore famine in that country, and he began to be in need.

text.NRSV: So he went and hired himself out to one of the citizens of that country, who sent him to his fields to feed the pigs.
text.Rotherham: And he went his way, and joined himself unto one of the citizens of that country, and he sent him into his fields to be feeding swine.
text.KJV: And he went and ioyned himselfe to a citizen of that countrey, and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.
text.NABRE: So he hired himself out to one of the local citizens who sent him to his farm to tend the swine.
text.DRB: And he went, and cleaved to one of the citizens of that country. And he sent him into his farm to feed swine.

text.NRSV: He would gladly have filled himself with the pods that the pigs were eating; and no one gave him anything.
text.Rotherham: And he used to long to be filled with the pods which the swine were eating; and, no man, was giving unto him.
text.KJV: And he would faine haue filled his belly with the huskes that the swine did eate: & no man gaue vnto him.
text.NABRE: And he longed to eat his fill of the pods on which the swine fed, but nobody gave him any.
text.DRB: And he would fain have filled his belly of the husks that the swine did eat; and nobody gave unto him.

text.NRSV: But when he came to himself he said, ‘How many of my father’s hired hands have bread enough and to spare, but here I am dying of hunger!
text.Rotherham: But coming, to himself, he said—How many hired servants of my father, have bread enough and to spare, whereas, I, with famine, here, am perishing!
text.KJV: And when he came to himselfe, he said, How many hired seruants of my fathers haue bread inough and to spare, and I perish with hunger?
text.NABRE: Coming to his senses he thought, ‘How many of my father’s hired workers have more than enough food to eat, but here am I, dying from hunger.
text.DRB: And returning to himself he said: How many of my father's hirelings have aboundance of bread, and I here perish for famine?

text.NRSV: I will get up and go to my father, and I will say to him, “Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you;
text.Rotherham: I will arise, and go unto my father, and will say unto him—Father! I have sinned against heaven, and before thee:
text.KJV: I will arise and goe to my father, and will say vnto him, Father, I haue sinned against heauen and before thee.
text.NABRE: I shall get up and go to my father and I shall say to him, “Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you.
text.DRB: I will arise, and will go to my father, and say to him: Father, I have sinned against Heaven and before thee:

text.NRSV: I am no longer worthy to be called your son; treat me like one of your hired hands.”’
text.Rotherham: No longer, am I worthy to be called a son of thine,—Make me as one of thy hired servants. And he arose, and came unto his own father.
text.KJV: And am no more worthy to be called thy sonne: make me as one of thy hired seruants.
text.NABRE: I no longer deserve to be called your son; treat me as you would treat one of your hired workers.”’
text.DRB: I am not now worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hirelings.

text.NRSV: So he set off and went to his father. But while he was still far off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion; he ran and put his arms around him and kissed him.
text.Rotherham: Now, while yet he was holding afar off, his father saw him, and was moved with compassion, and, running, fell upon his neck, and tenderly kissed him.
text.KJV: And he arose and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ranne, and fell on his necke, and kissed him.
text.NABRE: So he got up and went back to his father. While he was still a long way off, his father caught sight of him, and was filled with compassion. He ran to his son, embraced him and kissed him.
text.DRB: And rising up he came to his father. And when he was yet far off, his father saw him, and was moved with mercy, and running to him fell upon his neck, and kissed him.

text.NRSV: Then the son said to him, ‘Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son.’
text.Rotherham: And the son said unto him—Father! I have sinned against heaven, and before thee: No longer, am I worthy to be called a son of thine,—[make me as one of thy hired servants.]
text.KJV: And the sonne said vnto him, Father, I haue sinned against heauen, and in thy sight, and am no more worthy to be called thy sonne.
text.NABRE: His son said to him, ‘Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you; I no longer deserve to be called your son.’
text.DRB: And his son said to him: Father, I have sinned against Heaven & before thee, I am not now worthy to be called thy son.

text.NRSV: But the father said to his slaves, ‘Quickly, bring out a robe—the best one—and put it on him; put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.
text.Rotherham: But the father said unto his servants—Quick! bring forth a robe—the best! and put on him, and get out a ring for his hand, and sandals for his feet.
text.KJV: But the father saide to his seruants, Bring foorth the best robe, and put it on him, and put a ring on his hand, and shooes on his feete.
text.NABRE: But his father ordered his servants, ‘Quickly bring the finest robe and put it on him; put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.
text.DRB: And the father said to his servants: Quickly bring forth the first stole, and do it on him, and put a ring upon his hand, and shoes upon his feet:

text.NRSV: And get the fatted calf and kill it, and let us eat and celebrate;
text.Rotherham: And be bringing the fatted calf, sacrifice! and let us eat and make merry:
text.KJV: And bring hither the fatted calfe, and kill it, and let vs eate and be merrie.
text.NABRE: Take the fattened calf and slaughter it. Then let us celebrate with a feast,
text.DRB: and bring the fatted calf, and kill it, and let us eat, and make merry:

text.NRSV: for this son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found!’ And they began to celebrate.
text.Rotherham: Because, this my son, was, dead, and hath come to life again, was lost, and is found. And they began to be making merry.
text.KJV: For this my sonne was dead, and is aliue againe; hee was lost, & is found. And they began to be merie.
text.NABRE: because this son of mine was dead, and has come to life again; he was lost, and has been found.’ Then the celebration began.
text.DRB: because this my son was dead, and is revived: was lost, and is found. And they began to make merry.

text.NRSV: “Now his elder son was in the field; and when he came and approached the house, he heard music and dancing.
text.Rotherham: But his elder son was in a field; and, as, in coming, he drew near unto the house, he heard music and dancing,—
text.KJV: Now his elder sonne was in the field, and as he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard musicke & dauncing,
text.NABRE: Now the older son had been out in the field and, on his way back, as he neared the house, he heard the sound of music and dancing.
text.DRB: But his elder son was in the field, and when he came and drew nigh to the house, he heard music and dancing:

text.NRSV: He called one of the slaves and asked what was going on.
text.Rotherham: and, calling near one of the youths, he inquired what these things, could be.
text.KJV: And he called one of the seruants, and asked what these things meant.
text.NABRE: He called one of the servants and asked what this might mean.
text.DRB: and he called one of the servants, and asked what these things should be.

text.NRSV: He replied, ‘Your brother has come, and your father has killed the fatted calf, because he has got him back safe and sound.’
text.Rotherham: And, he, said unto him,—Thy brother, hath come, and thy father hath sacrificed the fatted calf, because, safe and sound, hath he received him back.
text.KJV: And he said vnto him, Thy brother is come, and thy father hath killed the fatted calfe, because he hath receiued him safe and sound.
text.NABRE: The servant said to him, ‘Your brother has returned and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf because he has him back safe and sound.’
text.DRB: And he said to him: Thy brother is come, and thy father hath killed the fatted calf, because he hath received him safe.

text.NRSV: Then he became angry and refused to go in. His father came out and began to plead with him.
text.Rotherham: But he was provoked to anger, and would not go in. And, his father, coming out, began to entreat him.
text.KJV: And he was angry, and would not goe in: therefore came his father out, and intreated him.
text.NABRE: He became angry, and when he refused to enter the house, his father came out and pleaded with him.
text.DRB: But he had indignation, and would not go in. His father therefore going forth began to desire him.
